summary: The pons is divided into a ventral basis pontis (containing pontine nuclei and transverse fibres) and a dorsal tegmentum (containing cranial nerve nuclei and ascending tracts).
The pons is entirely composed of white matter tracts.
The tegmentum is the ventral part and the basis pontis is the dorsal part.
The facial nerve (CN VII) fibres loop over the abducens nucleus (CN VI) to form the facial colliculus on the floor of the 4th ventricle.
The abducens nerve loops over the facial nucleus to form the facial colliculus.
The facial colliculus is located on the floor of the 3rd ventricle.
Pontine lesions often affect both CN VI and CN VII together because of the intimate relationship of CN VII fibres looping over the CN VI nucleus.
CN VI and CN VII are never affected together by a single pontine lesion.
CN VI and CN VII are located in different parts of the brainstem.
In locked-in syndrome, the patient is fully conscious but quadriplegic and mute, communicating only through vertical eye movements, due to bilateral ventral pontine destruction.
In locked-in syndrome, the patient is unconscious.
Locked-in syndrome is caused by cerebellar lesions.
The middle cerebellar peduncle is the largest peduncle and carries pontocerebellar fibres from the pontine nuclei to the contralateral cerebellar hemisphere.
The superior cerebellar peduncle is the largest peduncle.
The middle cerebellar peduncle carries fibres from the cerebellum to the thalamus.
The trigeminal nerve (CN V) emerges from the lateral aspect of the pons and has three divisions: ophthalmic, maxillary, and mandibular.
The trigeminal nerve emerges from the medulla oblongata.
The trigeminal nerve has two divisions.
